name: Copper King Mine
short_description: Historic copper and gold mine site in northeastern Washington with moderate-elevation terrain. Search the tailings and exposed rock faces for native copper, gold specimens, and colorful copper mine...
description:
The Copper King Mine is a historic mining claim located in Stevens County's rugged northeastern Washington landscape, near the small community of Goldfield Mill. This abandoned mine site sits at moderate elevation (2,369 feet) with rolling terrain typical of the Colville National Forest region. The site offers rockhounders the chance to explore old mine workings and adjacent areas where copper minerals, gold flakes, and secondary copper oxidation products can be found in tailings piles and weathered rock outcrops.

The geology here reflects Washington's complex mineralization history. The area sits within the Cascade-Sierra metallogenic province, where Mesozoic and Tertiary hydrothermal activity deposited copper-rich veins in metamorphic and granitic host rocks. The Copper King workings were likely established in the late 1800s or early 1900s during the region's mining boom, when prospectors systematically worked copper-bearing zones throughout the Stevens County hills.

Visitors should expect moderate difficulty terrain with uneven rocky footing and some steep sections near old mine cuts. The moderate slope and mixed forest/exposed rock environment means the site is accessible but requires sturdy footwear and basic scrambling ability. Old mine structures may be present; always stay alert around abandoned excavations and respect any posted claims or safety barriers.

This location is well-suited to intermediate rockhounders and experienced beginners with good field sense. The combination of documented mineralization history and accessible tailings makes it a rewarding destination for those seeking hands-on prospecting experience in the Pacific Northwest.
history: The Copper King Mine developed during Washington's late 19th and early 20th century mining era, when numerous copper and gold prospects were staked across the Colville district of northeastern Washington. The local geology features Mesozoic metamorphic rocks intruded by granitic plutons, which during regional hydrothermal activity created copper-bearing veins and disseminated mineralization typical of the Cascade Range metallogenic province.
tips: Focus your search on the tailings piles immediately around the mine portal and along the main pit walls where fresh rock is exposed; secondary copper minerals (malachite, azurite) often coat these areas in distinctive green and blue colors. Bring a hand lens and small chisel to inspect quartz veins and feldspar-rich rocks for native copper and chalcopyrite; look for brassy metallic surfaces and reddish oxidation stains. Late spring and early fall offer the best combination of dry ground and manageable temperatures; avoid heavy rain periods when muddy conditions make footing treacherous. Check for any active claims markers or posted restrictions before digging, and remove only loose specimens from clearly abandoned tailings to avoid disturbing any remaining claim integrity.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,369 feet with moderate slopes and uneven, rocky footing. Mixed conifer forest with exposed rock outcrops and old mine cuts. Walking distance from parking likely 0.25 to 0.75 miles depending on exact site layout. Terrain is not wheelchair accessible and requires sturdy boots with good ankle support.
best_season: May - September. Late spring through early fall offers dry conditions, accessible trails, and manageable temperatures in the northern Washington mountains. Winter snowpack and muddy spring conditions make access difficult or impossible.
written_directions: From Goldfield Mill, take Stevens County Road or Forest Service access roads northeast toward the Colville National Forest area. GPS coordinates 48.780966, -117.654436 should guide you to the mine site vicinity. Note that exact road names and current access conditions may vary; verify current Forest Service maps and local road conditions before attempting the drive, as remote forest roads in this region can be seasonal. High-clearance vehicle recommended for final approach.
road_conditions: Forest Service roads, likely unpaved and seasonal. Washboard and ruts probable. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Road may be gated or closed in winter.
parking_notes: Limited or informal pullout near mine site. No developed lot. Space for 2-3 vehicles likely. Exact parking conditions unknown; scout the site before committing to heavy equipment.
collection_rules: Personal use rockhounding only on National Forest land. Maximum 25 lbs per day per National Forest regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. Respect any active mining claims and posted restrictions. Stay clear of mine shafts and unstable structures.
rules:

nearest_services: Goldfield Mill is the nearest reference point; larger supplies (gas, food, water) available in Colville approximately 20-30 miles west.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and some steep grades near old mine cuts. No wheelchair access. Sturdy footwear with good ankle support required. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility. Scrambling ability helpful but not essential for tailings exploration.

faq.question: What minerals and gems can I find at Copper King Mine in Washington?
faq.answer: The primary finds at Copper King Mine are native copper, chalcopyrite (brassy copper-iron sulfide), and secondary copper oxidation minerals including malachite (green) and azurite (blue). Gold specimens may also be present in quartz veins. The colorful green and blue stains on tailings are often oxidized copper minerals worth collecting.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Copper King Mine free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Copper King Mine is free and requires no permit. It sits on National Forest land where personal use mineral collection is allowed. You do not need advance authorization, but you must follow National Forest regulations: maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial use, no motorized excavation, and respect for any posted claims.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Copper King Mine near Goldfield Mill?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season is May through September. Late spring and early fall offer dry ground conditions, accessible Forest Service roads, and manageable temperatures in the northern Washington mountains. Winter snowpack and spring mud make access difficult or impossible during other months.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Copper King Mine for beginners?
faq.answer: Copper King Mine is moderately accessible for experienced beginners and intermediate rockhounders. Terrain is uneven and rocky with moderate slopes, requiring sturdy footwear and basic scrambling ability. The main tailings areas are navigable without technical climbing skills. Children ages 8 and up can participate with adult supervision.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to Copper King Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small chisel or pry bar to loosen specimens from tailings, a hand lens to inspect rock surfaces for metallic luster and color, a sturdy backpack, plenty of water, and a good headlamp or flashlight if exploring near old mine cuts. Wear high-quality hiking boots with ankle support, gloves for sharp rock edges, and a hat for sun protection. Leave any excavation equipment at home - motorized digging is not allowed.

faq.question: Are there rockhounding locations closer to Goldfield Mill than Copper King Mine?
faq.answer: Copper King Mine is one of the documented historic mining sites in the Colville district near Goldfield Mill. Other copper and gold prospects exist throughout Stevens County's National Forest land. However, Copper King Mine's documented mineralization history and accessible tailings make it a primary destination. Always check current Forest Service maps and local conditions before visiting remote sites in this region.

meta_description: Hunt for native copper, malachite, and gold at Copper King Mine near Goldfield Mill, Washington. Free National Forest rockhounding on moderate terrain.
